facts_disclosed: Stopped university teaching to focus on fieldwork full-time

turns.content: I did something this week that has been sitting inside me like a stone and I need to talk about it

I resigned from the university. I told the department I would not be returning to teach the vernacular architecture seminar next semester. after all these years at la Chile, I walked in on Monday and let it go
That's a significant decision. How are you feeling now that it's done?

like I have set down something heavy and my arms do not know what to do with the emptiness. relief and grief braided together. every Tuesday I was leaving buildings in Yungay that were whispering their secrets to me so I could go stand at a podium and describe walls to students who had never touched them. I was offering photographs of water to people who had never been thirsty

That tension between direct experience and teaching about it sounds like it had been growing for a while. The more immersed you got in the fieldwork, the more the classroom must have felt like a translation of something that shouldn't be translated.

yes, exactly that. and there is a Neruda line that kept circling in my head — you cannot embrace the new thing if your hands are still gripping what came before. my hands were full of syllabi and grading and committee meetings. I needed them empty. I need them on the walls, on the plaster, reading the cracks with my fingers. not holding a laser pointer
Hands on walls instead of laser pointers — that's a clear choice. Did the department or your students react to the news?

the department head understood. she said I was one of the few who taught from experience rather than theory, which was kind and also confirmed my decision — the experience is where I belong. but one student, Valentina, she wrote me an email saying my seminar was why she chose heritage conservation as her path. that was the hardest part. that one kept me awake
That's heavy to carry. But Valentina's direction is set now — she doesn't need you in a classroom anymore. You gave her the spark. Your fieldwork might end up reaching people in different ways.

Diego said nearly the same thing. he said my students learned from me because I was doing the work, not because of the classroom. keep doing the work and they will find you. which is either wisdom or a very elegant way to help me not feel guilty. I suspect it is both

Probably both, and that's fine. Some of the most influential people in any field teach through their work rather than through lectures. Did you leave the door open to return, or was this a clean break?

I said the door is always open from my side. but I do not think I will walk back through it. Tuesday mornings are mine now. I will be in Yungay before dawn, coffee in hand, waiting for the light to touch the facades. the seminar was the photograph of the work — beautiful but flat. I want the three dimensions. the dust, the smell of old plaster, the cracks that tell you where the earth moved. inshallah that is enough

Tuesday mornings in Yungay before dawn sounds like exactly where you should be. You traded a podium for a courtyard, and from everything you've described about your fieldwork, that's a trade that will pay off in ways a seminar never could. The stone you were carrying — it sounds lighter now.
